did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically CNN, ResNet50, and VGG16, to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant for the early detection of skin cancer. The study trained these models on a dataset of 3,307 images sourced from Kaggle to obtain classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from training and evaluating deep learning models (CNN, ResNet50, VGG16) on a specific dataset of 3,307 skin cancer images. It includes a methodology section detailing data preprocessing, model architecture, and training parameters, followed by a results section with classification reports, confusion matrices, and accuracy metrics. Although published in conference proceedings (ICUIS-2024), it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection using Different Deep Learning Models
journal: Proceedings of the Fourth International Conference on Ubiquitous Computing and Intelligent Information Systems (ICUIS-2024)
brief_summary: This study investigates the application of deep learning models, specifically CNN, ResNet50, and VGG16, for the early detection of skin cancer by classifying lesions as benign or malignant. The researchers utilized a dataset of 3,307 images sourced from Kaggle, preprocessing them for compatibility with the respective architectures. The models were trained over 20 epochs, with performance evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and confusion matrices on a validation subset. ResNet50 and VGG16 achieved high training accuracies of 99.60% and 97.58%, respectively, while the custom CNN model demonstrated a validation accuracy of 84%. The results indicate that deep learning techniques, particularly ResNet50 and VGG16, show significant potential in assisting dermatologists with accurate skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as class 0 in the dataset; included in the 3,307-image Kaggle dataset used for training and validation. The paper notes benign tumors generally do not recur once removed.

Classified as class 1 in the dataset. The paper focuses on detecting skin cancer, specifically mentioning melanoma as a serious type in the introduction, and classifies lesions as malignant. Includes 150-151 cases in the validation set across models.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the models were trained to 'classify skin lesions as benign or malignant.' The dataset contains two categories: benign (class 0) and malignant (class 1). Performance was evaluated using classification reports (precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrices for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of skin cancer images sourced from the Kaggle online platform, uploaded to Google Drive and accessed via Google Colab. The images were converted to grayscale (224x224 pixels) for the CNN model and kept as RGB (224x224 pixels) for ResNet50 and VGG16.

use.training: Used to train the CNN, ResNet50, and VGG16 models over 20 epochs.
use.validation: Used to evaluate model performance (10% of the total dataset reserved for validation).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

CNN.training_accuracy_peak: Not explicitly stated as a single value, but validation accuracy was better than training accuracy before 12 epochs
CNN.training_loss_trend: Higher than validation loss in initial iterations, then lower after 10 epochs

ResNet50.validation_loss_trend: Went higher than training loss with proceeding iterations

Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: ResNet50 and VGG16 achieved high training accuracies of 99.60% and 97.58% respectively. The paper concludes that ResNet50 and VGG16 prove a better tool for detection of skin cancer compared to the custom CNN model. The validation set consisted of 331 images (180-181 benign, 150-151 malignant depending on the specific model's split variation or reporting). All models were trained for 20 epochs.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; ResNet50 and VGG16 are standard architectures often pretrained on ImageNet, but the paper does not specify if pretrained weights were used or if they were trained from scratch.

CNN.layers: Four convolution layers, four max_pooling layers, two dropout layers (20% drop rate), one flatten layer, two dense layers

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (standard cross-entropy implied by Softmax output)
training_details.data_preprocessing: Normalization by dividing pixel values by 255; CNN inputs converted to grayscale; ResNet50/VGG16 inputs kept as RGB

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion to grayscale for CNN model compatibility
Normalization by dividing pixel values by 255
Splitting dataset into training (90%) and validation (10%) subsets
conclusions: Deep learning models, specifically ResNet50 and VGG16, demonstrate high potential for assisting dermatologists in the early detection and diagnosis of skin cancer, leading to improved patient outcomes.

ResNet50 and VGG16 are superior tools for skin cancer detection compared to the custom CNN model, achieving higher training accuracies (99.60% and 97.58%, respectively) and better validation performance.
The custom CNN model achieved a validation accuracy of 84%, with a confusion matrix showing 157 true benign and 120 true malignant predictions out of 331 validation cases.
ResNet50 achieved a validation accuracy of 87%, with a confusion matrix showing 160 true benign and 129 true malignant predictions out of 331 validation cases.
VGG16 achieved a validation accuracy of 87%, with a confusion matrix showing 289 successful predictions out of 331 validation cases.
Early detection of skin cancer is critical because survival rates exceed 99% when diagnosed in early stages but drop to approximately 20% after the cancer spreads.
Machine learning techniques offer impressive performance in detecting skin cancer in preliminary stages compared to clinical diagnosis alone.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single-source dataset (3,307 images from Kaggle) without external validation, limiting generalizability to diverse clinical populations.
The dataset is highly imbalanced, with significantly more benign cases (180-181) than malignant cases (150-151) in the validation set, which may bias model performance.
There is a significant discrepancy between training accuracy (97.58%-99.60%) and validation accuracy (87%) for ResNet50 and VGG16, indicating potential overfitting despite the use of dropout layers.

The custom CNN model was trained on grayscale images while ResNet50 and VGG16 were trained on RGB images; this architectural inconsistency makes direct comparison of feature extraction capabilities difficult.
Key hyperparameters such as learning rate, batch size, and optimizer are not reported, hindering reproducibility.
Pre-training weights for ResNet50 and VGG16 are not specified; it is unclear if transfer learning was utilized, which is critical for performance on small datasets.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which is a limitation for deep learning models on small datasets to prevent overfitting and improve robustness.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests or confidence intervals for the reported accuracy metrics.
The validation set size (331 images) is small, making the performance metrics potentially unstable and less reliable for clinical deployment decisions.
